pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP) THE SMOKING GUN: The constellation of acute, unexplained neurovisceral symptoms (severe abdominal pain without surgical cause, motor neuropathy, severe psychiatric disturbance, and refractory hypertension/tachycardia) pointed strongly toward a defect in heme synthesis. Further questioning of the family revealed that the patient had recently been attempting a prolonged very low-calorie fast (stress/nutritional trigger) and, crucially, had started a new sulfonamide-based antibiotic for a minor skin infection (pharmaceutical trigger). A STAT porphyrin precursor assay confirmed massively elevated levels of Porphobilinogen (PBG) and Delta-Aminolevulinic Acid (ALA). PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: AIP is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by a partial deficiency of the enzyme Porphobilinogen Deaminase (PBGD), also known as Hydroxymethylbilane Synthase (HMBS). In most carriers, the condition is latent. However, induction of hepatic CYP450 enzymes (by drugs like barbiturates, sulfonamides, or through metabolic stress such as fasting, high estrogen levels, or alcohol) drastically increases the demand for heme synthesis. Because PBGD is deficient, the upstream neurotoxic precursors—ALA and PBG—accumulate in the bloodstream and tissues. ALA is thought to be directly neurotoxic, causing damage to the autonomic nervous system (leading to tachycardia, hypertension, and abdominal pain) and the central nervous system (leading to psychosis and seizures), as well as the peripheral nerves (resulting in the ascending paralysis). Treatment involves immediate cessation of the inducing agents and infusion of intravenous high-dose carbohydrate load (glucose) to suppress hepatic heme synthesis, often supplemented by hemin (Panhematin) infusion to replace the deficient heme and provide feedback inhibition.
